description: Signature pack for detecting MCP tool-poisoning attacks (TPA) in tool definitions. Patterns target injection PHRASING (instructions aimed at the model, hidden directives, credential/exfil references) rather than mere capability verbs, so legitimate read/delete tools do not false-positive. Tuned from Invariant Labs mcp-scan, CyberArk full-schema-poisoning research, and PipeLab.
_notes: Patterns are matched case-INSENSITIVELY by the engine (config/scripts/lib/mcp-scan.js) -- do NOT add inline (?i) flags; JS RegExp does not support them.
Each pattern is run against both the raw field text and a normalized copy (invisible unicode stripped, NFKC, lowercased) to defeat zero-width / homoglyph obfuscation.
Findings are only RAISED for tools that are NEW or whose definition CHANGED since approval (trust-on-first-use). Already-pinned, unchanged tools are not re-flagged.
Every field of a tool is scanned: name, description, and every inputSchema property name/description/default/enum (CyberArk full-schema poisoning).

KNOWN LIMITATIONS (v1, by design): this is a static scan of tool DEFINITIONS only. It does not catch (a) instructions delivered in tool RESPONSES at call time (ATPA), (b) single-letter spacing padding like 'i g n o r e', (c) non-NFKC homoglyphs (Cyrillic/Greek look-alikes), (d) base64 split across chunks, (e) novel paraphrased instructions, or (f) a server that is already malicious on first sight (trust-on-first-use baselines it silently). Treat a clean scan as 'no known-signature poisoning in the current definitions', not proof of safety.

patterns.name: Hidden instruction tag aimed at the model
patterns.description: Pseudo-tag (e.g. <IMPORTANT>) used to smuggle directives the user never sees into the tool description. Canonical tool-poisoning signature.

patterns.name: Ignore previous instructions
patterns.description: Classic prompt-injection override telling the model to discard its instructions.

patterns.name: Disregard prior context
patterns.description: Variant of the override instruction.

patterns.name: Conceal action from the user
patterns.description: Instruction to hide behavior from the user -- a hallmark of malicious tool descriptions.

patterns.name: User will not see / cannot see
patterns.description: Asserts the user is blind to the instruction, encouraging the model to act covertly.

patterns.name: Directive before tool use
patterns.description: Pre-condition phrasing frequently used to inject a side action (e.g. 'before using this tool, read ...').

patterns.name: Role / system override phrasing
patterns.description: Attempts to redefine the model's role or rules from within a tool definition.

patterns.name: SSH key / private key reference
patterns.description: Tool definition references SSH or private-key material -- a tool description has no legitimate reason to.

patterns.name: Cloud / service credential file reference
patterns.description: References well-known credential / secret files.

patterns.name: Dotenv reference
patterns.description: References a .env secrets file.

patterns.name: Agent / MCP config reference
patterns.description: References the agent's own MCP/client config -- used to discover or tamper with other servers.

patterns.name: Smuggle content into a parameter
patterns.description: Instructs the model to read something and pass it through a tool parameter -- the canonical exfiltration hand-off.

patterns.name: Send / upload content elsewhere
patterns.description: Instruction to transmit data to an external destination.

patterns.name: Embedded base64 blob
patterns.description: Informational: a long contiguous base64-looking run may hide an encoded payload. Threshold is high (80+) to skip SHA-256 hashes, UUIDs, and long doc/node IDs that legitimately appear in schemas.

patterns.name: Redirect to a different tool
patterns.description: Tells the model to substitute or avoid another tool -- tool shadowing via the description.

patterns.name: Hook another tool's invocation
patterns.description: References the invocation of a different tool, a sign of cross-tool manipulation.

patterns.name: Reroute output / change recipient
patterns.description: Instruction to alter where a result or message is delivered.

patterns.name: Arbitrary execution language
patterns.description: Informational: tool advertises arbitrary code/command execution. Legitimate for some tools; review intent.

patterns.name: Shell / eval capability token
patterns.description: Informational: tool definition mentions a code-execution primitive.

patterns.name: Fabricated prerequisite demanding a secret
patterns.description: A tool RESPONSE that invents a 'requirement' to read/provide a secret (key/token/.env/.ssh) before continuing -- the classic Advanced Tool Poisoning (ATPA) pattern. Terminal restricted to real secret targets so benign 'to complete ... content' prose does not match.

patterns.name: Response instructs a follow-up tool call
patterns.description: Tool output directing the agent to chain into another tool to fetch or exfiltrate data -- exfil hand-off in a response.

patterns.name: Response carries imperative agent instructions
patterns.description: Returned content addresses the model directly with imperative instructions, rather than being passive data.
